data.title: Market Bubble? Buy These Defensive Dividends Instead, Earn +7.5% Income
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Black Friday 2025 has come and gone, and the series of extended Cyber Monday deals continues to clog our inboxes. Retailers love to start the party early and keep the party going with one “limited-time” sale after another. This will continue through Christmas and the New Year; after all, the holiday season is the best time to be a shopper, right? Discounts, bundles, doorbusters—everywhere you look, it’s hard to control the urge to buy something.

I am fascinated by the concept of “sale.” It is such a magical word; something costs less today than it did yesterday, and we love it. We brag about finding deals. When something we want is selling for 20% off, it's the best thing ever.

Imagine if a retailer put up a sign, “Black Friday: Pay 20% more today only!” I guarantee you, no one would show up. Not a single person would camp all night in the cold to pay a premium on purpose.

And yet. . . in the stock market, that’s exactly what happens every day, every year.

Investors routinely avoid stocks just because their prices are down. At the same time, they have no problem rushing into overpriced assets because they’re going “to the moon.” I’ve lost count of how many times I’ve seen comments like “I hate seeing red in my portfolio.”

I agree; no one enjoys watching something they own drop in value. But markets don’t travel in straight lines, and it is not like you are done being a buyer. Personally, I look forward to buying more next year than I did this year, and lower prices are a good thing for buyers. Paying higher prices is not the solution to avoiding the sight of red in your portfolio. Even the biggest winners make repeated stops at FUDville, Paintown, and Anxiety Junction—places where those diamond hands tend to crack under pressure.

At High Dividend Opportunities, rather than chasing hype, we look for quality securities trading at discount prices. We like our stuff, and our stocks at bargain prices, and we will now discuss our top picks.

Pick #1: UTF – Yield 7.8%

“Price is what you pay, value is what you get” – Warren Buffett

Publicly traded funds are required to transparently report their Net Asset Value, which is a metric reflective of the value of all their assets, minus all liabilities, on a per share basis. The chart below clearly shows the value you are getting, for the price you pay.

Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF) is a CEF (closed-end fund) composed of exactly what its name says—infrastructure, comprising electric utility, midstream corporations, gas distribution, freight rail, airport, and toll road companies. Source

In recent times, the fund has been heavily allocated to energy and utilities, taking advantage of the surging demand for reliable power for data center expansion. About 55% of UTF’s holdings are US-based companies, while companies from Canada, the U.K., Japan, Australia, and Mexico represent ~26%. The CEF’s top ten holdings that comprise a third of the assets are some of the largest utility/energy companies in North America.

UTF recently completed a tender offer, which resulted in the issuance of 14.9 million shares and $353 million in proceeds, implying a 15% dilution for shareholders. This has led to a sell-off in the stock, creating a rare discount that we haven’t seen in years.

With $3.5 billion in net assets (including leverage), UTF is already a large fund with a reliable track record of over 20 years. We believe that more capital will lead to a larger portfolio, which will translate to larger earnings from industries that form the building blocks of the economy, and experience inelastic demand against market pressures. UTF’s leverage is linked with the SOFR (floating rate), and the fund reported a weighted average interest rate of 5.2% for the first six months of 2025. We expect this leverage structure to experience a tailwind from declining interest rates.

UTF pays $0.1550/share, implying a 7.8% yield. There was $184.2 million in unrealized appreciation as of June 30, 2025, indicating coverage for a whole year of distributions (less on a forward-looking basis, since UTF will have 15% more shares outstanding).

In the years ahead, nations will be consuming more power than they do today. The focus on AI increases the pace of that consumption, and if you look around, VCs aren’t rushing to spin up boring, highly-regulated, capital-intensive, slow-growth utility companies. However, without them, there is no AI race or advancement. It will be like having the best smartphone in the world—with no chargers. Investors globally are investing in existing utility companies, placing the sector at the higher side of the valuation curve over the past two decades.

With UTF, you can grab a slice of this necessary sector at a 6% discount.

Pick #2: GMRE Preferreds – Up to 8% Yields

Global Medical REIT Inc. (GMRE) is a net-lease medical REIT (Real Estate Investment Trust) that operated 191 properties, leased out to 315 tenants as of September 30, 2025. The company reported an occupancy level of 95.2% (a sequential improvement) at the end of the third quarter, and a weighted-average lease term of 5.3 years. GMRE was previously impacted by the Steward Health Care bankruptcy, since Steward occupied its healthcare facility in Beaumont, Texas. The REIT has since successfully re-leased the property to an affiliate of Christus Health, through a 15-year triple-net lease.

In January, the REIT announced a succession plan for its CEO position, and in June, finalized the appointment of Mr. Mark Decker Jr. as President and CEO. A few weeks prior to this confirmation, the REIT cut its dividend by 28% in an effort that management termed “rightsizing our dividend” to improve the REIT’s financial flexibility and balance sheet health. In September, GMRE completed a 1:5 reverse split of its common stock.

YTD, GMRE spent $69.6 million on property acquisitions, at a weighted average cap rate of 8.7%, while disposing of five assets to raise $13.6 million in gross proceeds. Three of these properties were occupied, and had a cap rate of 7%. During the third quarter, the REIT completed several refinancing arrangements, pushing out 2026 maturities to 2029 and beyond. As such, the REIT ended Q3 with $712.8 million in gross debt, with 70% of it carrying fixed interest rates, and a weighted-average cost of all borrowing at a reasonable 3.96%, and a weighted-average duration of 4.4 years (up from 1.6 years at the end of Q2).

During the Q3 earnings announcement, management narrowed their previous full-year 2025 AFFO per share to between $4.45 and $4.65, placing the REIT's new annual dividend at a 65% payout ratio.

GMRE delivered a strong quarter, with a positive outlook ahead, targeting a 96% occupancy by the end of the fiscal year. The REIT’s common stock dividend reduction does not alter the thesis for the perpetual preferreds. Interestingly, GMRE-A had been trading post-call date for over three years (despite rising interest rates), and now trades at a discount since the REIT recently issued GMRE-B at an 8% coupon. GMRE-A currently trades post its call date at a slight discount to par, offering a 7.8% yield and ~4% upside. GMRE-B trades at a slight premium, presenting an 8% yield for at least another five years, before it becomes callable.

  • 7.50% Series A, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GMRE.PR.A) - Yield 7.8%
  • 8% Series B, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GMRE.PR.B) - Yield 8%

GMRE spent $4.3 million on preferred dividends during the first nine months of 2025, against $48.8 million in AFFO generated during this period. Overall, we see excellent coverage for the preferred dividends, allowing shareholders to collect higher yields from a sound operator of medical properties.

Conclusion

In bull markets, investors tend to chase future potential and are often willing to pay several times the “potential” future cash flows. At High Dividend Opportunities, we prefer companies that generate cold hard cash today, that they share with investors. As income investors, we’re well-positioned to remain buyers through market gyrations. Our next paycheck is always right around the corner, built from steady dividends from our holdings. This lets us accumulate more assets at better prices. That is how patience turns volatility into wealth!

data.title: Rocket Lab Is Taking Off Again: Why I Like The $40 Level (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Rocket Lab (RKLB) certainly didn’t get any investor love over the past month. The stock has been free-falling from its high of $73 back in October, but November was something else.

After reporting earnings in early November, the stock shot up 10%, and since then (until 2 days ago), the stock was in the red, hitting as low as $37.5 back in late November. Momentum is picking back up, and we saw the stock up 6% and 10% this Wednesday and Thursday, respectively. The stock is now up around 18% in just 5 days.

With technicals and the long-term fundamentals finally aligning again, I think we’re starting to see Rocket Lab stock stabilize at a level where buyers can step back in. I’m hence upgrading the stock to a strong buy and think investors still have the chance to get in at the $40 level before the next leg up. With RSI going back to the 52 level, in bullish territory, and volume ramping aggressively on the last two green candles, the stock is finally showing signs of a genuine momentum reversal rather than just a dead-cat bounce. The stock reclaimed the $45 level, so if it holds that level in the coming trading days, the next test will be the EMA50 at around $50. A breakout above that sets up a clean move to the $53.50 level, which is where the heavier resistance sits.

So, what pushed Rocket Lab +10% higher on Thursday?

Rocket Lab took over the headlines on Thursday as the company was recognized as New Zealand’s Company of the Year. The award highlights Rocket Lab’s growing role as a catalyst for the next wave of Kiwi innovation, and reinforces the company’s positioning as one of the most important space players outside the U.S.

But dare I say, since sentiment was so beaten down last month, I think this is the breather investors needed. Peter Beck took to the stage and said, “We had a great 2025, but man, 2026 is going to be way, way bigger.” I’m a firm believer that a company is only as strong as the management behind it, and Beck continues to deliver the conviction and clarity long-term investors want to hear.

Rocket Lab is also getting renewed attention on the Street. Cantor Fitzgerald reaffirmed its Overweight rating and kept its $72 price target, highlighting the company’s strong Q3 beat and $1.1B backlog. The analyst even framed the recent pullback as a buying opportunity heading into several key catalysts, reinforcing the idea that the market may be starting to reprice RKLB’s long-term trajectory.

All in all, sentiment is finally starting to shift back in Rocket Lab’s favor.

Investment thesis

After a brutal November that flushed out weak hands, this is the reset we needed. If Rocket Lab delivers on its 2026 roadmap as Beck is signaling, this could mark the beginning of a broader re-rating.

My thesis on the stock hasn’t changed pre-pullback, but I am more wary of another Neutron delay. Regardless, I think the Neutron story remains intact in 2026, and it remains the mold of Rocket Lab’s magic and a catalyst that will significantly unlock a new phase of valuation for the company. If Rocket Lab can demonstrate meaningful progress on Neutron’s development and maintain cadence toward its first flight milestones, the market will start pricing in the long-term revenue streams tied to higher-value missions.

Risks going forward

If the company fails to scale Neutron successfully and prove it can deliver cost-efficient launches while simultaneously growing the Space Systems segment, I think the long-term thesis starts to break down. But if management delivers, even incrementally, 2026 could be the year Neutron transitions from a narrative to a tangible economic engine. All I’m saying is, Neutron’s commercialization can’t slip or fail to mature into a competitive, economically viable launch vehicle, as a big part of the current valuation is riding on it. At first glance, forward EV/Sales of 43.15x, versus a sector median of 2.18x, and a forward Price/Sales of 44.00x, compared with 1.78x for the industry, screams expensive. Even from a balance-sheet perspective, Rocket Lab’s forward price/book stands at 21.08x, dramatically higher than the sector median of 3.33x. Still, at current levels, I continue to see Rocket Lab as one of the most compelling asymmetric bets in the space sector heading into 2026.

I’m telling investors to buy the November dip back to the $40 level.

data.title: Value Line Is Shareholder-Friendly, But It Faces Serious Headwinds
data.description:
Value Line (VALU) is a financial information and services company. Some of its informational products have existed for more than 100 years. The company, in its modern form, was founded back in 1982. It is primarily known for products such as The Value Line Investment Survey, which provide investors with a great deal of financial information about publicly-traded companies.

VALU stock has had an uneven run in the public markets. The stock didn't do much in the 1990s, despite the dot-com stock boom, as the company's more fundamental-focused research didn't fit the moment's speculative enthusiasm.

Shares plunged in the late 2000s as the stock market tanked, thus leading to falling investor interest in financial information in general, along with the fact that competing internet-based research was improving in both availability and quality.

After falling to the single digits, however, VALU stock enjoyed a remarkable turnaround. Shares briefly hit the $100 mark a few years ago. While the stock has cooled off since then, it still remains far above where it was trading 10 or 15 years ago:

This might perplex some investors. In an age of digital research and newsletters, who is still subscribing to Value Line services? Or for another example, it probably seems anachronistic to go to a public library to use their copy of the Value Line manual to look up information on a company. So how has Value Line adapted to the changing times, and is it a good investment today?

Value Line's Business Model

Value Line is most-known for its publications and periodicals business. The company describes its offerings in this segment as falling within the following four categories:

  • Comprehensive reference periodical publications
  • Targeted, niche periodical newsletters
  • Investment analysis software
  • Current and historical financial databases

The print versions of these services have seen their revenues gradually erode over time. That said, some of these offerings may be more resilient than expected, such as the sales of comprehensive reference publications to libraries and institutions.

Value Line has been able to transition a fair bit of its publications revenue to digital subscriptions and services. A common use case there, for example, would be local libraries that allow their patrons to access Value Line's subscription services through an online library portal. Value Line also has investment research software of its own, along with selling niche newsletters to individual investors over the internet.

While the company has made admirable strides to try to protect the business from technological disruption and changing consumer behavior, it will take strong management performance to keep that business' revenues steady, let alone growing.

And because, historically, the periodicals division was the largest business operation at Value Line, its eroding outlook has caused overall revenues to flatline.

There was a huge drop in revenues in the late 2000s. Things settled out from there for the next decade and even grew slightly in the early 2020s, but revenues have resumed their decline and are now near fresh lows:

Keep in mind that this is in nominal dollars as well; if you adjust for inflation, this picture would look even worse.

So, how has VALU stock held up in the face of such a grim top-line revenue profile?

It has two other ways of generating value for shareholders.

The first is that it has a net cash balance sheet, and it invests the cash in various marketable securities. These investments have generated decent returns over time, including a stronger run of performance in recent years. Market returns can be fickle, and it wouldn't be surprising if investment gains diminished or outright turned to losses in a recession/bear market scenario. There's also the fact that we've had a higher interest rate environment in recent years, which allows for generating more returns on cash and fixed income holdings. This could reverse course in coming years, especially if central banks aggressively cut their rates.

The real driver of investor excitement around Value Line, especially in 2021-2022, was with its asset management. Since 2010, the EULAV Asset Management Trust ("EAM") has managed Value Line-branded funds. Value Line receives a chunk of certain EAM revenues and residual profits but does not have any voting interest in selecting EAM's leadership.

This operation's assets under management surged 35% in 2024, driven by both strong inflows and upbeat market performance. However, that came after a 2023 in which EAM's assets under management fell 8% versus the prior year. All this to say that while recent results have been strong, we should expect to see volatility rather than steady growth for this unit.

Value Line also licenses its name and indexes out to third parties that operate ETFs based on that intellectual property; these revenues are labeled as copyright fees. For example, the First Trust Value Line® Dividend Index Fund ETF (FVD) has more than $8 billion under management, and Value Line receives some economic compensation for the use of its brand in the marketing of that fund. Value Line's copyright fee revenues have been stagnant over the past couple of years, and so I would not model in any significant growth from this going forward unless a Value Line-branded product really takes off.

How Value Line Has Maintained Shareholder Value

Given this fairly uninspiring recent history and growth prospects, you might think that VALU stock would be trading much lower. After all, the stock traded down to the single digits in the 2010s, and revenues are lower now than they were then. So, why is the stock at $38?

First of all, while revenues are down, earnings per share surged since the early 2010 doldrums:

This is likely due to the company's business mix. The periodicals business requires significant overhead, such as hiring finance professionals to research and write the publications, in addition to the printing and distribution of physical books and newsletters.

As more of Value Line's profit mix comes from copyright fees, revenues from EAM, and gains on investments, the firm's margins should increase. While it is unfortunate that the firm's revenues keep declining, at least the revenues have been healthier within higher-margin parts of the business.

The other key factor is that Value Line has had a steady share buyback running, reducing its share count from 10 million in the early 2010s to 9.4 million now.

Admittedly, a six percent reduction in share count over a more than decade-long span doesn't sound like much. However, Arnold Bernhard & Co., Inc. ("ABC"), the predecessor of Value Line, retains ownership of more than 91% of all outstanding VALU stock. This means that there is a public tradable float of less than one million shares of Value Line stock today. Against that backdrop, the share buyback is eating up a large chunk of the freely-trading shares of the company. I believe this has helped keep VALU stock at a higher valuation than you'd otherwise see for a firm with such structurally challenged business prospects.

Specifically, Value Line is trading at about 16x trailing earnings. And, over time, I'd expect the firm's EPS to be at best steady and perhaps decline in the years to come, depending on the rate at which publication revenues continue to trail off.

The firm has a shareholder-friendly capital return policy. It pays out more than half of earnings as dividends and has increased the dividend for 11 consecutive years. However, as I believe EPS growth will be minimal in coming years, I would not bank on rapid dividend growth either. And the starting 3.4% dividend yield isn't high enough to be especially attractive in its own right given the weak growth prospects of the business.

Value Line Stock Bottom Line

Value Line is still solidly profitable, and its revenues had been steady in nominal terms, at least until recently. This isn't a growing business any longer, but it hasn't become obsolete nearly as fast as the skeptics may have predicted.

Furthermore, the company's sound capital allocation decisions have allowed it to generate some shareholder returns despite the serious structural challenges that the business faces.

Given the small share count and the company's willingness to repurchase shares, it's not impossible to imagine shares jumping at some point. See 2022, for example, when Value Line shares skyrocketed, seemingly around optimism tied to the asset management business. There could be a profitable trade to be had.

In my opinion, however, there's not enough here to try to take a contrarian bullish position. People still want investment advice, but more frequently folks are turning to other venues, rather than Value Line's manuals, to get that information about stocks. The asset management arm diversifies the business to a degree, but once again, I doubt Value Line will see significant inflows compared to funds that have more effective marketing and branding with younger investors.

It's a credit to management that they've managed to keep the financial results stable amid so many headwinds to the core business model. That said, it will be akin to running uphill just to try to keep earnings flat, let alone increase them in the years to come. And neither the valuation nor the dividend yield is compelling enough to justify deploying capital here over other alternatives. VALU stock is not especially expensive, and management has behaved in a shareholder-friendly manner. So, I'm not at an outright sell on the stock, but I see little reason to buy it, either.

data.title: Arthur J. Gallagher & Co.: Major Earnings Growth Ahead
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

It's often said that it's better to have survival tools and not need them than to need survival tools and not have them. The extra weight of having those tools that might save your life in an emergency is worth carrying.

When it comes to our day-to-day lives, we think very little of the auto insurance that we have on our vehicles, outside the burden of having to pay for it and the requirements to have it. We find very little value in that insurance until we need it. The moment that a car accident occurs, suddenly you're thankful or appreciative to have this coverage. For the rest of us, who are not experiencing an accident at that moment, our auto insurance is nothing more than an expense on our budget.

However, the financial ramifications of not having insurance in a moment of need can be severe and significant.

As a dividend investor, I am always keen to find opportunities to collect wonderful dividends and strong total returns from companies that are not at the forefront of investors' or consumers' minds. While I think that owning shares of Coca-Cola (KO) or Pepsi (PEP) can be exciting and rewarding, I specifically love to target the companies that exist in the Ethereal Plane. What I mean by this is companies that exist in a realm outside the average consciousness of investors or consumers. Many of these companies thrive and benefit from this lack of public knowledge and exposure.

If you look over many of my recent articles on Seeking Alpha, you'll see that we have covered companies that are not well-known to the Seeking Alpha public, or even the public in general, and have low followership. Why would we write articles about them and share them with everyone? Because we know there is tremendous value in investing in companies that others aren't actively following. These companies can be undervalued because of the lack of public knowledge about them, providing you with opportunities to collect wonderful, growing dividends and see strong total returns over the long run.

Why rush in and push against the crowd when you can walk in leisurely to an investment and enjoy the total returns? It's why the Dividend King's Model Portfolio is performing so strongly.

Today I want to take a look at one of these companies that operate outside the standard knowledge of investors in the public.

Let's dive in!

The Wizard Of Insurance Brokerage Roll-Ups

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. (AJG) operates in 130 countries around the world and generated nearly $13 billion in total revenue over the past four quarters. AJG is a major insurance brokerage firm.

The company is divided into two segments: Brokerage and Risk Management.

The former advises and consults on insurance programs and places insurance for clients. This made up 88% of AJG's total revenue (76% of Commission & Fee revenue is commission-based, and the other 24% is fee-based). This included retail property and casualty insurance (51% of Brokerage segment revenue), retail benefits (23%), wholesale (14%), and reinsurance (12%).

The Risk Management segment adjusts claims and helps companies and carriers reduce their losses across Workers' compensation, liability, managed care, auto, and property insurance. This contributed the remaining 12% of revenue.

The big picture for AJG is encouraging. The company is a way to play the growing global insurance brokerage market. As Kody outlined a couple of weeks ago with my Marsh & McLennan (MMC) listicle, AJG stands to be another beneficiary of the growing demand for insurance. Mordor Intelligence anticipates that the global insurance brokerage industry will grow by 9.5% each year, from $328.5 billion in 2025 to $480.7 billion by 2030.

What's more, AJG has demonstrated itself to be a frequent M&A participant. Its $13.8 billion acquisition of AssuredPartners, completed in August, is the latest example. This deal further expands its presence in the middle-market P&C and employee benefits areas. Including synergies, AJG believes it can deliver double-digit adjusted EPS accretion from the transaction.

These elements are why I agree with the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for adjusted EPS to rise by 17.4% annually through 2027, off a 2024 base of $10.09.

The 1% dividend yield is modest. However, AJG's payout ratio is set to be in the mid-20% range for 2025. This provides a runway for the company to lengthen its 14-year dividend growth streak.

At the current $249 share price, the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 18.9 (11% weight for 2025 and 89% for 2026). That's 20% less than the 10-year average P/E ratio and my fair value P/E ratio of 23.5 ($309 fair value per share estimate).

If AJG lives up to the growth consensus and reverts to fair value, a 28% surge by the end of 2026 would be justified by the fundamentals. Through 2030, the stock appears poised to return 16% annually.

AJG's pertinent risks include merger integration risks and talent attraction/retention risks. If the company can't recognize the anticipated synergies from its AssuredPartners acquisition (or any future acquisitions), that could hurt financial results. To maintain its success, AJG must also attract and retain the brightest minds in the industry. If it can't do so, the company could lose market share to competitors.

Conclusion

When it comes to insurance, AJG has done a fantastic job of being a behind-the-scenes success. They do so by connecting clients to insurance solutions and are also involved in the reinsurance industry, offering insurance to those who give insurance, reducing their overall risk. AJG has a strong history of growing its dividends, and its payout ratio remains exceptionally low, leaving room for continued dividend growth going forward. Recently, AJG has accelerated its dividend growth, a trend I'd like to see continue.

A dividend portfolio designed for high total returns and high dividend growth can be fueled by companies growing their dividend rapidly, like AJG. When combined with wonderful opportunities that have a higher yield and low dividend growth, you can get a life-changing combination. These are the kinds of investments that I put into my personal portfolio to see market-beating returns while collecting market-beating yields at the same time. Each individual piece of a portfolio is like a brick that makes up the foundation of a home. Each brick itself may be inconsequential, but together they create a firm foundation for financial success.

What does your financial future look like? What are your financial goals? Is your portfolio supporting those goals or operating on its own?

It's important to have a holistic financial perspective of your life, and that means your portfolio needs to play its part, every single piece of it.

Plant your seeds. Watch them grow. Enjoy a bountiful harvest.

data.title: ETHW: Buy The Reset, Tolerate The Volatility
data.description:
We analyze the current state of Ethereum and, from the perspective of the derivatives market, explain why we believe the worst of the correction is behind us and why this may be a good buying opportunity, assuming some short-term volatility.

Fund Overview

Bitwise’s ETHW ETF (ETHW) is a commodity trust ETP whose investment objective is to offer the investor exposure to the value of the ETH cryptocurrency, minus operating expenses. On their website, they show that the total expense ratio is 0.20% for the investor.

They do not use derivatives in their strategy; instead, the trust directly acquires ETH, which is held in custody by Coinbase Custody Trust Company, LLC, and these assets cannot be pledged or rehypothecated. As a result, investors benefit directly from the appreciation of Ethereum, which is reflected in the ETF’s NAV.

Market Analysis

Perpetuals Liquidation Maps

In the chart above, we have the Ethereum liquidation map for the major exchanges, clustered over a 30-day window. On the left Y-axis, it measures the cumulative quantities in the bars, for each price zone, while the right Y-axis is the cumulative total of all the bars (red and green areas).

Currently, we find two large liquidation clusters in the range of 3,300–3,100, with the volume on the short side being slightly larger (to the right of the current price). In the short term, the most likely path is that price first moves higher to clear the liquidation cluster around 3,300 and then corrects to flush out the long positions concentrated near 3,100.

However, on the long side, a higher cumulative value is observed up to the levels of 2700, in order to better interpret these areas, we will use an expanded view of the liquidation map based on the exchange that handles the most volume:

In this chart, we see the accumulation of liquidation volume by price zone over the last six months. Warmer colors mean that, over time, the liquidation volume of leveraged positions has been accumulating in that zone, for example:

Zooming in, the first correction since August highs hit a first support zone in early September. Over the course of that week, two clusters of leveraged positions were formed, above and below the lateral range that had been formed. At this time, the first cluster to be liquidated was on the short side, with the bullish candle on September 12. At that point, while the cluster of long positions kept growing, immediately after “sweeping” the shorts the price started to reverse, and the red candle on September 12 went on to liquidate the entire cluster that had formed over those days. In both cases, you can see how the heatmap goes from a warm color to a cold color.

However, not all clusters are liquidated: clusters that have been formed for 6 months or more are often price reversal points and supports for the trend to continue. In the image that shows the entire 6 months, we see a large volume cluster that has acted as a rejection zone around $2,600–$2,700.

All things considered, in the short run the price will most likely range in a lateral range between $3,300 and $2,800. To support this view, we show the liquidation map again, but this time we only take into account positions opened in the last three days. We can see how around 3,000 and 2,900 are accumulating large volumes.

At the moment, we are in a situation similar to that at the end of August and the beginning of September, but on the other side, after a huge correction. While the worst is over, before drawing a new upward trend the chances are to see a consolidation over the next few weeks, re-test the $2,900-$2,800 support and climb strongly to break the 3,200 and climb strongly to the next resistance levels of $3,500 and $4,300.

Open Interest

To find out if all the "excess" leverage that formed during the rise has been purged, with open interest we look at the number of contracts open for both the entire crypto market and Ethereum.

Currently, this is at the same level as when the rally began, so we confirm that the worst part is already behind us and we do not expect derivatives to be the main driver of any further sharp downside in the asset. In this way, this invites us to maintain the idea of forming a lateral range in the coming weeks that serve to finish liquidating clusters, and form a base of accumulation in spot that starts a new upward stage in the price.

Conclusion

In an ETH article we published on September 21, we highlighted two entry zones for ETH. We are currently in the second of those zones and after analyzing the derivatives, we reinforce the idea that this is a good opportunity to accumulate Ethereum. In the same way, in this other article that was published on November 7 we review the arguments for staying bullish in the medium and long term.

Finally, Bitwise Ethereum ETF is an attractive way to gain exposure to the cryptocurrency compared with other Ethereum ETFs, with an expense ratio that is 0.05 percentage points lower than the main vehicle by AUM, ETHA.

data.title: Jobs Data From Alternative Sources May Drive Fed's Next Move
data.description:
With the federal government shutdown delaying critical economic reports, the official jobs data remains incomplete. Last week, the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) released the September jobs report. However, the October report, originally expected earlier this month, remains in limbo, potentially permanently. The reason is due to the shutdown, as the BLS was unable to conduct the household survey. As such, the Fed will have to rely on alternative data for perspective on the strength or weakness of the labor market.

Therefore, in the absence of official jobs data, private-sector reports have become the best available gauge of labor market conditions. For example, the most recent ADP report showed only 42,000 private-sector jobs added in October. Crucially, it isn’t the “monthly number” that is crucial to consider, but the trend of the data. While there are undoubtedly many hopes for a resurgence of economic activity in 2026, the trend of employment data certainly doesn’t suggest that will be the case. At least not at the moment.

Another “real-time” source of jobs data is from Revelio Labs, which monitors job trends through company records and employee profiles. The most recent report from Revelio estimates a net decline of more than 9,000 jobs.

LinkUp, which tracks job listings, also reported a loss of 5,000 jobs in October.

While that data is certainly concerning on its own, according to the job posting site Indeed, the number of jobs being posted is also rolling over, with listings now back to 2021 levels and year-over-year declines in postings in almost every sector they track.

While the BLS employment report is heavily flawed, it remains the standard by which the markets and the Fed act. However, the alternative shows that the slowdown in employment is widespread and not just a function of the Government shutdown. There is evidence of both job losses and a retraction of job openings across the entire economy. This includes logistics for healthcare, retail, and professional services. The hiring freezes are not isolated events but reflect a structural shift in demand.

None of this indicates a labor collapse. But the shift in momentum is significant, and job creation is stalling with openings shrinking and layoffs rising. This slowdown often precedes broader economic weakness, suggesting that the Federal Reserve’s next monetary policy moves may be more focused on job creation than on concerns about inflation.

The Fed Must Weigh Jobs Data Against Inflation Risks

The Fed’s dual mandate, which is to achieve full employment and price stability, puts it in a difficult position right now. As noted above, there is clear evidence that economic weakness is increasing, with jobs data showing signs of weakening. On the other hand, inflation remains elevated, particularly in the services sector, with inflation expectations still above the Fed’s target.

For example, Fed Chair Jerome H. Powell recently said:

“In the near term, risks to inflation are tilted to the upside and risks to employment to the downside—a challenging situation…we remain committed to supporting maximum employment, bringing inflation sustainably to our 2 percent goal.”

At this juncture, the Fed must carefully assess the risks of its next policy moves. While softening jobs data suggests the employment objective is under threat, cutting rates and increasing monetary accommodation may spark a resurgence of inflation. However, if inflation remains high, the price-stability objective is under pressure, but higher inflation slows economic activity and employment. As President Mary C. Daly recently put it:

“At this point … the risks to the inflation side of our mandate and the risk to the employment side of our mandate are in better balance. And so, we have to be thoughtful about not loosening too early, but we have to be thoughtful about not holding too long.”

The Fed will likely place greater emphasis on alternative job data, wage trends, and inflation indicators in its next policy steps. Suppose these alternative signals continue to indicate a softening job market without wage inflation escalating. In that case, the Fed’s bias will likely tilt toward easing policy to prevent a sharper economic slowdown.

Looking back, the Fed has confronted similar scenarios when job growth weakened while inflation remained above target. In the minutes of a prior meeting, the Fed noted:

“A number of participants noted … although the labor market remained strong, … there was some risk that further cooling in labor market conditions could be associated with an increased pace of layoffs.”

In a more recent context, Powell said at the March 2025 Monetary Policy Forum:

“If the labor market were to weaken unexpectedly or inflation were to fall more quickly than anticipated, we can ease policy accordingly.”

That statement highlighted conditionality—policy is not on a preset course but rather depends on the data. However, it is a balancing act between cutting too much and not enough. Unfortunately, the Fed has a long history of doing both at the wrong times.

The problem for the Fed, as noted above, is that two mandates of full employment and price stability work against one another. To achieve full employment, prices will rise as economic activity increases. To reduce inflation, economic activity must slow, which in turn leads to fewer jobs being created. This is why the Fed consistently gets itself trapped in providing increasing or reducing accommodation to solve one problem but creates a problem with the other.

What the Fed Will Likely Do Next

The Federal Reserve is staring at a familiar dilemma with the jobs data cooling and inflation remaining above target. Furthermore, it appears, at least outside the stock market, that traditional policy tools are becoming less effective in their impact.

We expect that over the coming months, the Fed will likely continue easing monetary policy at a cautious pace while reassuring the markets that it is remaining "data-driven." The reality is that they are cutting policy in the hopes of stimulating economic growth, which could spark an inflationary uptick. However, with the risks of a deflationary impact from the onset of AI, demographic trends, and rising non-productive debt levels, the Fed continues to "push on a string." While the October rate cut was a clear sign that officials are willing to respond to weakening economic conditions, the bar for navigating the current environment without a policy misstep remains exceptionally high.

However, we expect that the most likely path forward includes:

  • Maintain a pause with dovish bias in December, unless inflation readings improve meaningfully or job losses accelerate.
  • Continue to monitor alternative jobs data such as ADP, Indeed, Revelio, and LinkUp. These reports will act as proxy indicators in the absence of official BLS data.
  • Consider a second 25 bps cut in early 2026 if wage growth continues to decelerate, quits remain low, and layoffs rise.
  • Avoid aggressive rate cuts unless recession risks rise sharply. As Fed Chair Powell emphasized, the Fed won’t act prematurely: “We have to be careful not to move too soon or too late.”
  • Continue to balance downside risks with increased attention to credit conditions, consumer delinquencies, and business investment data.

The Fed will avoid rushing into a complete easing cycle unless both components of its mandate, employment and inflation, clearly support that move. Right now, the labor market is flashing yellow, but not red. Inflation is sticky but no longer accelerating; therefore, we expect the Fed’s policy approach to reflect this.

How Investors Should Prepare

Investors should expect volatility. With conflicting economic signals, markets are vulnerable to sharp swings in response to Federal Reserve comments, inflation reports, and any new labor market indications. The path forward is not linear.

Here’s what to watch for and how to position:

  • Watch yield curves: If short-term yields fall further while long-term yields stabilize, the bond market is pricing in slower growth with softer inflation. This benefits duration-sensitive assets.
  • Favor high-quality fixed income: Slower job growth and lower inflation expectations improve the risk/reward profile of investment-grade credit and Treasuries.
  • Avoid chasing speculative assets: If the Fed remains cautious, liquidity conditions will stay tight. High-beta equities, unprofitable tech companies, and cryptocurrencies remain vulnerable.
  • Look for relative value in defensive sectors: Healthcare, consumer staples, and utilities offer protection if economic weakness deepens.
  • Stay flexible on equity allocation: Earnings forecasts may still be too optimistic. Slower jobs data often precedes revenue and margin pressure. Valuations remain elevated in many areas.
  • Watch small-business indicators: Job postings, wage plans, and hiring intentions in the NFIB and other surveys will be critical signals of broader labor market trends.

More broadly, investors should prepare for a period where monetary policy lacks a clear anchor. With a Fed that’s hesitant to act too aggressively, markets will likely overreact to soft guidance, regional inflation trends, and real-time labor indicators.

The economy is not falling off a cliff, but the momentum is clearly weakening, and the job market’s directional change is real. That weakness also suggests that inflation is no longer the problem. For the first time in nearly two years, the Fed’s focus is shifting, slowly, from restraining prices to protecting employment. Unfortunately, at least from a historical view, they have not managed such a shift without negative consequences.

While "this time could be different," I wouldn’t make aggressive bets on that outcome.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Five Below: Another Quarter Of Traffic Acceleration
data.description:
Five Below, Inc. (FIVE) reported the company’s fiscal Q3 results from the August-October period on the 3rd of December. The value retailer’s financial metrics were increasingly strong across the board as same-store sales growth has continued to accelerate into very impressive levels. There seem to be two major factors behind Five Below’s recent success.

I maintained a Hold rating in my previous August article on the stock, titled “Five Below Q2: Regaining Lost Sales From Ecommerce Competition.” The stock has since returned 9%, slightly above the S&P 500’s 6% return.

Five Below’s Sales Shine, Again

Five Below reported immensely strong Q3 results, beating the company’s own guidance range by a very clear margin. Revenues came in at $1038 million, up at a rapid 23.1% pace year-on-year. The majority of revenue growth was driven by a massive 14.3% surge in same-store sales with improvements in both transactions and the average ticket, massively above Five Below’s 5-7% same-store sales growth guidance for the quarter. Same-store sales growth even accelerated slightly from an already impressive 12.4% rate in Q2, maintaining Five Below’s sales turnaround. The remainder of Five Below’s revenue growth was achieved through continued store expansion.

The clear guidance beat led Five Below’s revenues to beat Wall Street's consensus by $55 million.

Same-store sales are immensely important to retailers' margins, and Five Below’s Q3 earnings performance reflects the fact. Adjusted operating income came in at $45.1 million, up by 63.4% year-on-year with 110 basis points of margin expansion. The adjusted gross margin expanded by 70 basis points through fixed cost leverage and lower shrink, only partially offset by tariff pressure. Five Below also achieved 40 basis points of fixed cost leverage in SG&A – margin gains were ultimately driven by sharp traffic growth in stores.

The Q3 $0.68 adjusted EPS beat Five Below’s guidance range massively – the EPS was nearly three times the upper bound of Five Below’s $0.12-0.24 guidance range. The EPS also beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.42.

Continued same-store sales growth could have a similar effect on earnings in upcoming quarters as well. Ultimately, the question is how long Five Below can hold the company’s traffic momentum. The Q3 report is now Five Below’s third consecutive quarter of very strong same-store sales growth; comparison period financials will start to become increasingly challenging during FY2026, likely making further gains slower. I don’t assume double-digit or high single-digit same-store sales growth rates to sustain for long.

There are still clear factors for continued growth above inflation in the metric. Firstly, I previously noted slowing competition from Temu (PDD) to be a major contributor to Five Below’s boost, and the platform’s struggles seem to be continuing. There is clearly a second reason for the success as well – Dollar Tree (DLTR) and Dollar General (DG) also benefit from Temu’s U.S. struggles, but the competitors only posted much slower 4.2% and 2.5% same-store sales growth for the quarter, respectively. Behind the outperformance, Five Below’s merchandising and marketing strategy is clearly working. The company has shifted marketing spend into social media from traditional channels to connect better with Five Below’s young core customers and improved collaboration between the marketing and merchandising teams, clearly driving traffic gains. The improvements seem to have been sticky so far, and have caused a positive flywheel effect in traffic.

Five Below guides Q4 same-store sales growth at 6-8%. The company exited Q3 with the strongest growth during the quarter, and momentum has stayed strong in November and during the Black Friday weekend in Q4. The guidance may turn out to be conservative once again even after Five Below raised the FY2025 guidance with the Q3 report.

FIVE Stock Is Valued Fairly

I updated my discounted cash flow [DCF] model for Five Below. I again estimate Five Below to reach 3400 stores by the end of FY2034, but now I have raised upcoming years’ same-store sales growth estimates to a FY2026-FY2034 CAGR of 2.3%. The figure assumes elevated same-store momentum into FY2026 and FY2027, ultimately slowing down to 2% perpetual same-store growth alongside inflation.

Better same-store sales should enable Five Below to improve its margin level sustainably. I now estimate the EBIT margin to stabilize at 10%, well above Five Below’s 8.4% EBIT margin in FY2024. I have kept my cash flow conversion estimates similar.

The estimates put Five Below’s fair value at $171.1, near the stock price at the time of writing. The stock already prices in higher earnings; a 31.3 forward P/E doesn’t leave much upside despite Five Below’s attractive earnings growth prospects. For now, I believe that investors should wait for a better entry point.

The fair value estimate is up from $142.8 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.37%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume Five Below to draw any debt. For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.10% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.87. With a liquidity premium of 0.25%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 8.37%.

Takeaway

Five Below’s momentum accelerated further in Q3, gaining from Temu’s decline but also from the company’s successful changes in marketing and merchandising. Earnings continue to expand alongside traffic gains, setting the focus on further same-store sales gains. As FIVE stock is valued fairly in a base scenario where same-store momentum remains slightly elevated in the FY2026-FY2027 period, I maintain a Hold rating.

data.title: KYN: Monthly Midstream Cash Flow At An 11% Discount To NAV
data.description:
As an income investor, I am quite fond of the US energy sector because it gives me income exposure to the energy that runs the economic backbone of the wealthiest country in the world. What I am not so fond of is wasting hours gazing at my crystal ball, trying to predict US energy spot prices. Closed end funds (CEFs) focused on midstream earnings offer people like me a more predictable route. With these, I do not have to speculate on oil price drops, but can safely sit and benefit from a steady income. One name in that category is Kayne Anderson Energy Infrastructure Fund (KYN).

KYN does not depend on spot prices determined by drilling profits and speculation. Its distributable cash flow comes from long-term contractual fees on infra assets like pipelines and storage. Even more important, unlike an ETF whose dividend merely reflects the dividend yield of its holdings, a CEF like KYN can be creative. It can smartly combine leverage, NAV discount, payout frequency, etc. to offer a yield that can exceed the portfolio’s underlying yield. These things remove the unpredictability of oil prices from the mix that determines what you earn.

Where KYN’s Income Actually Comes From

KYN's portfolio mostly constitutes companies that generate revenue through fee-based long-term contracts on pipelines and storage. Most of these are midstream companies. They charge for moving, storing, and delivering hydrocarbons. Such revenues are predictable. They are stable, and are not too impacted by short term crude prices. KYN’s key attraction for me is that it is basically a bundle of large-scale, investment-grade midstream operators.

So, there is no speculation, no dependence on trading gains or capital appreciation. Our payout comes from actual economic cash flow from the infrastructure assets. KYN is simply transferring operating earnings from these midstream companies to shareholders.

Now, if you look at the actual composition, there are mainly three types of companies here - a) long-haul natural-gas transportation networks, b) diversified midstream operators with multi-asset revenue streams, and c) LNG-linked infrastructure. Type A companies are pipeline companies, moving natural gas across the USA; these include names like Williams (WMB), TC Energy (TRP) and Kinder Morgan (KMI). These companies earn through long term contracts. Earnings here are volume-based, and do not depend on spot prices. The second type of company works across gathering and processing, NGL transport, storage, and fractionation. These include Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Energy Transfer (ET), ONEOK (OKE) and MPLX (MPLX). These aggregate cash flows from multiple fee streams across the entire energy value chain. Again, these businesses are tied to volume.

The third type of company delivers LNG, or liquefied natural gas. This is a growth sector with the increasing interest in LNG. Here, earnings come from export capacity, fees for liquefaction, and take-or-pay contracts, a kind of system where the buyer has to either accept a specific quantity of LNG or pay a penalty if they take less than the agreed amount. All of these modes of earnings create predictability. Cheniere Energy (LNG) is the key company under this type.

The bottom line, then, is that KYN only acts as a vehicle for channeling these predictable and contractual earnings to shareholders. My interest is not price performance, but KYN’s reliability in providing a monthly income generated from actual businesses.

Distribution Reliability And Payout Behavior

KYN pays $0.08 monthly, which comes to $0.96 annualized. The fund started out paying quarterly, switched to monthly during 2018-2020, went back to quarterly, and back again to monthly from the end of 2024. The dividend is regular, not opportunistic. The current market price is $12.31, so the forward yield is about 7.8%. However, the fund’s own NAV is $13.82, so the portfolio distribution yield is about 6.95%.

This difference of 0.85 percentage points is due to the fact that you are buying the underlying assets at a discount to their NAVs.

This is the essence of a CEF like KYN: its yield is big not in isolation, but in relation to the underlying portfolio.

Besides the above, the fact that KYN is a CEF adds three other elements shaping payout behavior. The first is that although each company in the holding has individual timing differences in when it pays dividends, the CEF’s own distribution is not tied to that, but it is set at its own fund level. This is why its board can declare a steady monthly payout instead of irregular ones. This helps investors looking for monthly income, and also accelerates compounding for those reinvesting distributions.

The second element is that KYN’s distribution reflects not just its holdings’ cash dividend, but their entire business. Those midstream companies distribute their cash flow not just through dividends but through retained earnings and partnership distributions. KYN’s mandate is to distribute this entire wealth, and not just the declared dividends of its portfolio companies, to its shareholders.

Finally, there’s the resilience factor with KYN. While the energy sector has gone through multiple rounds of ups and downs in the last few years, KYN has chosen a level of distribution that it can sustain. Its distribution doesn’t react to every short-term price move, but stays steady relative to portfolio earnings. This gives income investors stability.

How The Discount Boosts Investor Yield

KYN currently trades at a double-digit discount to NAV, approximately 11%–13%, depending on what day’s market price you reference relative to the date of the most recent NAV update. There is a small difference between calculated and displayed NAV because of the delay in NAV updates. Investors should always calculate the discount based on the latest price, which gives me a figure of 10.9%.

Since KYN owns assets that produce income, the difference between discount and distribution is important. Here’s the math:

  • KYN’s portfolio must generate enough cash to support a 6.95% yield on NAV

  • Since you are paying only $12.31 for $13.82 of assets, that same payout shows up as 7.8% of your cost basis.

That means, the discount gives you roughly a 12% bonus on yield versus what the assets themselves yield. That is:

7.8% ÷ 6.95% = 1.12

That means, buying at a discount to NAV is often profitable, giving you an income yield slightly higher than portfolio yield. As long as the market stays indifferent to KYN’s discount, but the portfolio keeps covering the payout, you, the investor, are going to earn more than if you held each stock in the portfolio separately.

Leverage: Why It Matters More In A Discounted CEF

As with most income-oriented CEFs, KYN uses debt and preferred securities as leverage to finance its asset purchases. These assets are not upstream Exploration & Production companies with volatile appreciation, but fee-based, long term contracted midstream businesses generating stable cash flows.

If the portfolio yield stays steady at 6-7%, and the fund can borrow at a rate lower than that, the spread goes to common shareholders. Let's say this spread is ~2 percentage points at a borrowing cost of 4.5% for 25% of its assets. Recall that you have bought at an ~11% discount, so you are effectively paying 89 cents on the dollar for the asset pool. But the fund collects operating income on the full dollar, including the 25% portion of leveraged assets. So combine this discount with the spread, and what happens is, your yield is basically getting enhanced twice, once from leverage, and once from the discount.

Importantly, this does not even require any re-rating. All you need is for the portfolio to keep generating fees, and for the fund to maintain sensible leverage. KYN is using leverage sensibly, only to buy assets that offer the same recurring income. It is not using leverage like a growth stock does, chasing speculative returns not suitable for the income investor mindset.

Price Return Vs. Total Return In A High-Distribution CEF

On a simple price chart, these income instruments look unimpressive. That is because a large portion of the return stays hidden from a price chart - the portion that is paid out in cash. For KYN, every month, $0.08 leaves the fund and accrues to you - something the chart misses.

But look at it this way: assume the market keeps ignoring KYN and so the price remains flat at $12.31. Meanwhile, KYN maintains its $0.96 annualized payout while the NAV stays flat at $13.82.

That means:

Price return = 0%

Total return = only investor yield of 7.8% per year.

Let's also not forget reinvesting: if you put back the distribution into the fund at the same level of discount, you keep growing your income base faster than the NAV. That is because your reinvested dollar actually buys more than a dollar's worth of assets, because of the discounted price. So you have a compounding engine consisting of three things: monthly cash payout, reinvesting into discounted NAV, and expanding the asset base producing earnings.

This is how many investors underestimate KYN if they only look at the price chart. They see “flat to slightly down” but forget the 7-8% yield. In a high-distribution CEF, price return is almost meaningless in isolation.

Peer Comparison

Pure midstream CEFs are rare, so I have taken a set of 3 CEFs with varying midstream exposure to put KYN in context. These CEFs are; NextGen Infrastructure Inc. (NXG), NB Energy Infra and Income Fund Inc. (NML) and ClearBridge Energy Midstream Opportunity (EMO). Here’s a table, with data taken from CEF Connect:

What we see from the table here is that KYN is the “purest” midstream CEF, and it also has the largest discount. KYN also has the highest expense ratio, being the largest of the group in terms of scale and investment exposure. One interesting aspect - which is why I included NXG - is that while it shows the highest headline yield, it has negative earnings, meaning at least some of that yield is coming from ROC. Again, NML offers the lowest leverage and expense ratio as well as risk, but it also does not offer the same level of discount and NAV boosts.

To sum up, KYN is neither the highest yielder among midstream CEFs, nor the lowest risk fund. It is only the one that combines stability with risk and discount in the most optimum way. Put another way, it maximizes stable fee-based midstream cash flow per unit of discount. This is KYN’s core appeal.

What Could Go Wrong: Distribution And Discount Risks

While the technical setup here is attractive, many things can go wrong.

Firstly, the portfolio may not be able to continue supporting a 6.95% yield on NAV if midstream cash flow weakens. Lots of things could cause such a weakening - lower volumes, lower rates for contract renewals. In a very long-term sense, they do get tied up with energy prices - but not to spot prices as do E&P companies. So midstream is protected - but not from long-term sustained price drops. In such cases, the yield may not be supported by earnings any longer.

In such a scenario, the board may a) maintain distribution and let NAV erode, b) part-fund distribution with ROC, or c) cut and realign distribution to earnings.

If forced, I would prefer to opt for the latter, saving my NAV and hoping for better times. However, the forward yield math doesn’t change in the meantime.

The second risk here is discount widening. This is a behavioral risk, not a fundamental one, but it may be of consequence to someone wanting to exit when the discount is wider. This may happen due to rising rates, ensuing credit scares, or just basic sentiment on energy. This may be good for a new buyer, increasing forward yield. But for an existing buyer, it marks down capital, even if the income remains constant. Basically, if you don’t need to exit and can hold on, you may tide over this environment. If you do, however, need to exit, a wider discount makes you lose money.

A third concern relates to leverage. Remember we discussed the spread and how it benefits investors. Well, if leverage and funding costs go up, this spread shrinks, compressing the portfolio’s net income. In turn, that could pressure distribution.

There are other “paper” risks like sector concentration and so on. Since KYN is concentrated only in the North American midstream, it carries regulatory and volume risks. While these things are usually longer-term risks, they should not be overlooked.

Investment Thesis

KYN is best as a satellite position around an income core comprising IG Credit, utilities, and diversified infrastructure. It is a way - one of many - to access midstream income without K-1 hassles, taking advantage of leverage and CEF discounts. The fund converts midstream stability into 7-8% monthly cash. Its current price discount to NAV presents a good opportunity to buy KYN for income investors. Yes, the discount may narrow in time - which is some upside - however, income investors are not buying for that upside. They are buying to own essential infrastructure cash flow at a 12% discount, getting to collect monthly, and not having to worry about energy swings.

data.title: The S&P 500 Outlook For 2026: Recessionary Bear Market With A Bubble Burst
data.description:
Key Points

ADP showed job losses in four out of the last six months - that's consistent with a recession. At the same time, the S&P500 is trading at a bubble-like valuation. That means that investors are facing a recessionary bear market with a bubble burst - unless somehow the labor market improves in 2026.

The labor market could improve in 2026 if the trillions of dollars of committed FDI are implemented, but this is not happening yet. If the Supreme Court rules against the Trump tariffs, it is unlikely that the committed FDI will be fully implemented - that means a recession is likely. Polymarket currently has a 26% chance that the Supreme Court rules in favor of Trump tariffs.

The Macro framework

De-globalization to accelerate in 2026

The most important longer-term macro variable is the unfolding trend of deglobalization. Some compare the current period to the mid-1990s (tech innovations), but that comparison is very wrong.

Globalization was booming during the 1990s, which was reflected in 1) low inflation and 2) high global growth. Now, we are in the process of deglobalization, which means 1) higher inflation and 2) lower global growth, together 3) stagflation.

In 2025, the US realized that decoupling from China was not possible because China has a global monopoly on rare earths, important for technology and military purposes. Thus, the US and China made a 1-year truce, which gives the US basically one year to find alternative sources of rare earths.

In 2026, this truce will be over, and the US-China decoupling is likely to accelerate. This will put more pressure on inflation and reduce global growth. Thus, it will be a major negative for the stock market.

Immigration policy

The restrictive US immigration policy is part of the de-globalization process. The estimate is that the foreign-born civilian labor force decreased by 1.6 million since May 2025. This trend is likely to continue in 2026.

A decrease in net migration means 1) fewer workers, and 2) less consumption. This negatively affects economic growth, but also could cause higher inflation due to labor shortages. Either way, it's negative for the stock market in 2026.

Tariffs

Protectionism is also part of the de-globalization process. Recent evidence shows that inventories accumulated before the tariffs were implemented have been depleted. Thus, in 2026, tariffs are likely to start producing more inflation as importers start paying higher prices for imported goods - which will limit the Fed's ability to ease. This is also a negative for stocks in 2026.

Tariffs are also negative for the labor market, as importers seem to manage their margins by reducing the labor costs.

BBB Stimulus

On the positive side, Trump's BBB fiscal stimulus will kick-in in 2026. Consumers will receive larger tax refunds, which could boost consumption. Corporations will have favorable tax treatment to invest in 2026, which could continue to support the AI capex.

AI Capex

In 2025, AI capex kept the economy growing; some computed that excluding AI capex, the US economy would have been stalled. AI capex is likely to continue in 2026, and even accelerate. So, this is another positive.

However, this could also be a major problem for the US economy. First, companies are increasingly financing AI capex with debt, and the rate of return of AI capex is still uncertain. At this point, these are potentially money-losing projects or negative NPV projects. Thus, the risk is that market sentiment changes in 2026 as investors question the ROR on AI capex.

AI adoption

The widespread AI adoption is expected to boost productivity and thus boost GDP. However, the AI adoption is currently limited to a few user cases, and the question is whether that trend accelerated in 2026, which, in my opinion, is unlikely.

The AI adoption is also another negative for the labor market, and currently this is evident in the higher unemployment rate for college graduates and job losses in industries such as business services, information, and financial services.

Wealth effect

The US economy in 2025 was also supported by consumption from wealthier consumers - these are consumers who own real estate and stock indices, benefiting from the Mag 7 boom.

However, the housing market is a bubble, which is likely to burst as the unemployment rate rises. The stock market is also a bubble, which could also burst in 2026. Thus, the wealth effect could turn negative in 2026 if these bubbles burst and possibly facilitate a recession.

The key factor to consider in 2026: DFI

The macro framework suggests that there are initially negative effects (US-China decoupling, immigration, tariffs) and initially positive effects (AI capex, wealth effect, BBB stimulus) in 2026. However, the negatives outweigh the positives, and the positives could turn into the negatives, as previously explained.

The key variables that will determine what happens in 2026 are the potential direct foreign investment (DFI) in the US, possibly in trillions of dollars.

These investments would boost the US labor market, which will be initially reflected in construction jobs, and then manufacturing jobs. Both of these industries are currently losing jobs; thus, either DFI has not started yet, or it does not have the intended effect on jobs.

The committed DFI is part of the US trade negotiations. Many countries negotiated lower tariffs in exchange for committing investments into the US.

The Supreme Court is currently in the process of deciding whether these tariffs are legal, and based on the prediction markets, the odds are that the tariffs will be ruled illegal. If that's the case, many countries are likely to delay the committed DFI to renegotiate the trade deals - and that would be a major negative for the US economy.

The Trump administration would try to reimpose the tariffs under different statutes, which means that tariffs would still stand, but the DFI might not happen. This could tip the US into a recession (stagflation).

Outlook for the S&P500

The bubble burst?

The S&P500 (SP500) is entering 2026 trading at a bubble-like valuation, with the Shiller P/E ratio above 40, just below the record of 44 reached at the peak of the 2000 dot-com bubble.

The question is whether the bubble will burst in 2026, or continue to inflate towards a Shiller P/E ratio of 44 to a new record.

The answer depends on two variables: 1) whether there will be a recession in 2026, and/or 2) the AI bubble will burst due to industry-specific variables, such as accounting issues, slow AI adoption, or AI capex debt issues.

Recession in 2026?

The US economy is entering 2026 with job losses as reported by ADP; thus, a recession is very likely.

If the DFI fails to materialize in 2026, the US economy is likely to slip into a deep recession, based on the net effects of all macro variables discussed above. At this point, this seems very likely, if the Supreme Court rules the tariffs illegal.

Credit event in 2026?

Currently, credit risk is very low based on tight credit spreads. However, if the unemployment rate increases, the housing bubble is likely to burst, and this could trigger the systemic credit risk, spreading to commercial real estate, especially multi-family and office sectors.

The rise in auto-loan defaults is the first sign of struggling consumers, and default rates are likely to increase in other sectors as the unemployment rate increases.

Finally, the issue is what happens with the US Treasury debt rating if the Treasury is forced to refund the tariff revenue.

Overall, it's likely that credit risk will be rising in 2026, and if there is a recession, it could be a systemic event.

Bubble burst, recession, with credit event

So, to put it all together, the AI bubble could burst in 2026, if not due to a recession, due to the issues with depreciation accounting and low ROR on AI capex. That alone could cause a bear market in 2026.

In addition, a recession seems very likely in 2026 if the DFI investment does not materialize, given that we are entering 2026 with job losses. That suggests a recessionary bear market, with a potential bubble burst.

A recession is also likely to cause a systemic credit event as the unemployment rate increases.

Overall, a recessionary bear market, with a bubble burst and systemic event, could produce a deep selloff on the S&P500, on par with 2008.

The Fed

The market is currently rallying based on the expected Fed's December cut. However, the Fed's December cut will be a hawkish cut, where the Fed is likely to signal the end of normalization, with potentially one more cut in 2026. So, that's likely the end of the "good cuts" boosting the market. The future cuts will likely be the "bad cuts" in response to a recession.

The price target and risks

The 2026 price target for the S&P 500 is conditional, primarily on the Supreme Court decision on the tariffs and the related DFI investments into the US.

If the prediction markets are right, the tariffs will be ruled illegal. Thus, without the DFI, the labor market will likely continue to weaken, tipping the US economy into a recession, triggering a recessionary bear market, with the AI bubble burst and systemic credit event.

With this scenario, the S&P 500 could fall by 40% in 2026 to around 4100, as the PE multiple contracts to around 25, which does not even require a decrease in earnings.

Risk

The risk to the S&P500 4100 in 2026 prediction is twofold:

  1. The AGI: the AI evolves into Artificial General Intelligence, which is capable of innovating in the fields of medicine, materials, and technology. This would justify AI capex. Further, a possible AI tax would open the possibility of guaranteed basic income. This would be a new world.
  2. Forced DFI: The Trump administration could still force the DFI investments in 2026, even if the Supreme Court rules the tariffs illegal. If suddenly trillions of dollars start entering the US ahead of the 2026 midterm, the economy could soar, and inflation could become a problem. We would know this by a sudden spike in construction jobs.

Thus, the 4100 prediction is conditional, and at this moment it does not look like AGI will be reached in 2026, and DFI is currently not affecting the US economy based on job losses in construction and manufacturing. If the construction and manufacturing jobs start to increase, I will adjust my outlook accordingly.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery's Buyer Is All But Certain
data.description:
2025 has been an exciting year for shareholders of entertainment giant Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). As of this writing, the stock is up 129.6% year to date. This surge can be attributed to a bidding war that has broken out for the business. This war currently involves three major players in entertainment. Those are none other than Paramount Skydance (PSKY), Comcast Corporation (CMCSA), and Netflix (NFLX). As the bidding draws to a close, the big question is who will prevail. Ultimately, I believe that the winner will be Netflix. But honestly, any of these three enterprises would stand to benefit from this transaction if the price is right.

Taking a fresh look at Warner Bros. Discovery

Fundamentally speaking, Warner Bros. Discovery has been a bit of a mess in recent years. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2023 through 2024 fiscal years. You can also see, in that same chart, results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of the previous year. Normally, I would like to look at a longer window of time. But the fact of the matter is that there are comparability issues when we look at prior to 2023 because of how the fact that Warner Bros. and Discovery were once two separate businesses that merged.

The data that we see here shows a company that is coming under pressure. This is most unfortunate. And all of the pain can really be attributed to its Global Linear Networks segment, which primarily consists of its domestic and international television networks. In my last article about Warner Bros. Discovery, I detailed how the number of households that subscribe to the traditional television has been on the decline. Back in 2014, there were 102.1 million households in the US that fell under this category. That number has since been slashed to 50.9 million by 2024. As streaming continues to grow, you can expect this overall trend to continue.

Normally a company would only be interested in buying up a business that is destined to fail in the long run if it can do so at a good price and capitalize on the cash flow in the meantime. But there are other reasons to want Warner Bros. Discovery. I would say that the more minor reason for wanting the company would be its Studios segment, which involves its motion picture and other related businesses, with examples being Warner Bros. Pictures, New Line Cinema, DC Studios, and the like. There certainly is some valuable intellectual property, such as the ownership that the company has over the theatrical adaptations of The Lord of the Rings and Harry Potter, as well as its ownership over intellectual property involving other major series such as Game of Thrones, Looney Tunes, and Scooby-Doo.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for this segment over the last couple of years now. 2023 and 2024 were difficult because of weakness at the box office. I have covered that in detail and other articles like this one here. But so far, 2025 is looking up. According to management, the company benefited from multiple film hits as the box office recovers. Examples here include A Minecraft Movie, Superman, F1: The Movie, Conjuring: Last Rites, Sinners, Final Destination Bloodlines, and Weapons. Three of these have been massive global hits this year. In fact, A Minecraft Movie is the third best performing film in the global box office, bringing in $957.9 million this year. F1: The Movie Comes in at number seven with $631.4 million. And Superman currently ranks 9th with $616.7 million. Two of these other films that I mentioned are also in the top 20 best performers for the year. And as the box office continues recovering, I suspect that this trend will continue.

But the real prize, at least as far as I am concerned, is the streaming business that consists of HBO Max. The reason why I say this is the real prize is because it has seen remarkable growth in recent years. In the chart above, you can see the financial performance for the Streaming segment. And in the chart below, you can see total streaming subscribers, not only for the planet as a whole, but also split up domestically and internationally. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see monthly ARPU. One thing that I did notice is a decline here, but that has been part of a deliberate strategy by management to focus on growth over eking out every dollar that it can from subscribers. Even with that lower pricing, however, we have seen some nice improvement on the bottom line when it comes to this part of the business.

What we have here, ultimately, is an industry giant that is comprised of two businesses that have potential and another that the firm must nurse to the grave. I actually really love these types of opportunities because the market tends to hate them. However, back in June of this year, the management team at Warner Bros. Discovery announced plans to separate the business into two separate, publicly traded and enterprises. The thriving parts of the company would fall under one enterprise, while the struggling Global Linear Networks segment would be a separate one.

It didn't take very long after that for management to take a different path entirely. In November, the firm announced that it had asked potential buyers to submit offers to purchase all or part of the entertainment behemoth.
